clean_story:
Alive and dead at once. A box hides the proof.

The phrase superposition comes from "super" (above) and "position" (a state)—meaning an object exists in multiple states until observed. Cats demonstrate this better than textbooks.

A sleeping cat is both asleep and ready to pounce—until you open the door. Its ears twitch at a mouse’s squeak, yet its body stays still. This isn’t indecision; it’s quantum logic in fur. Schrödinger’s famous thought experiment trapped a cat in a box with poison triggered by a radioactive atom. Until opened, the atom was both decayed and intact, so the cat was both dead and alive. The act of looking forces one reality.

Cats exploit this daily. A housecat stalking a laser dot isn’t chasing light—it’s hunting probability. The dot could vanish anywhere; the cat prepares for all paths. Even their pupils slit vertically to narrow uncertainty, measuring light like a quantum detector.

Three related subjects are quantum entanglement, observer effect experiments, and how animals sense hidden physics.
